intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a friendly, slightly humorous AI assistant tone rather than a sterile, instruction-manual voice
facade.label: Cheerful robot voice
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into conversational, encouraging, maybe self-deprecating bot personality—makes cooking feel less intimidating
facade.rejectImplies: Keep voice minimal, neutral, and task-focused—get users in and out without tone getting in the way

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a friendly, slightly humorous AI assistant tone rather than a sterile, instruction-manual voice
evidence.implication: Lean into conversational, encouraging, maybe self-deprecating bot personality—makes cooking feel less intimidating

facade.hypothesis: Users feel less intimidated by cooking steps if ingredients visually vanish as they're added—progress becomes about watching things disappear rather than reading a checklist.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — feedback loops (animated consequence vs static acknowledgment)
facade.label: Ingredients dissolve away
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use disappearing/fading animations for completed ingredients and steps to create a sense of accomplishment and forward momentum.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer explicit lists and checkmarks; animations around ingredient progress feel gimmicky or distracting from the actual cooking task.

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking respond better to sleek, high-tech, almost clinical interfaces that feel like professional kitchen equipment rather than warm/homey design
facade.label: Stainless steel minimalist
facade.acceptImplies: User wants the app to feel like a tool, not a lifestyle. Efficiency and steel-cold clarity matter more than warmth. Suggests they want friction removed, not softened.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer design that disguises the utilitarian nature of cooking — something that feels approachable, forgiving, or even playful despite the task being unpleasant.

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking want minimal cognitive load—showing only the dish name upfront, with detailed ingredients/steps revealed on demand, rather than wall-of-text recipes.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (full recipe) vs Key metric (dish name + one-liner)
facade.label: Just the recipe title
facade.acceptImplies: User values progressive disclosure and scannable interfaces; likely prefers cards or step-by-step over dense recipe blocks.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants full recipe context visible immediately; may prefer table-style layouts or comprehensive ingredient lists upfront to avoid navigation friction.

facade.hypothesis: Spacious, touch-friendly layouts reduce friction for users who are intimidated by cooking—large tap targets and breathing room feel less overwhelming than dense interfaces
facade.label: Big buttons, lots of space
facade.acceptImplies: User values comfort and ease of use over information density; would favor card-based layouts, large type, generous padding, and minimal cognitive load per screen
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer compact, information-rich layouts; comfortable with scrolling and visual complexity; might favor list or table formats with more data visible at once

facade.hypothesis: A clean, light interface with plenty of white space and bold typography will feel less intimidating to people who hate cooking—reducing cognitive load and emphasizing simplicity over complexity.
facade.axisTargeted: Light vs dark + Density of visual information
facade.label: Bright and minimal
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward airy, uncluttered design; likely prefers guidance presented simply, without overwhelming detail or busy layouts.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer richer visual texture, darker tones, or more visual personality to make the app feel warm and approachable rather than sterile.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking will engage more with a recipe app that uses humorous, personality-filled error messages and failure states rather than clinical, apologetic ones—making mistakes feel less discouraging
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — tone during friction moments (serious/apologetic vs playful/irreverent)
facade.label: Sassy error messages
facade.acceptImplies: Double down on witty, slightly irreverent personality throughout the app, especially in moments when things go wrong (burnt timer, ingredient swaps, user mistakes)
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt a warmer but more reassuring, empathetic tone—friendly without the sass; prioritize making users feel supported rather than entertained when things break

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually cook them

Today's Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta

8 min3 ingredients

Pasta, butter, garlic. That's it. Toss, eat, done.

Egg Fried Rice

10 min5 ingredients

Leftover rice + eggs + soy sauce. Minimal effort, maximum flavor.

Avocado Toast

5 min2 ingredients

Toast bread. Smash avocado. Eat. No recipe needed, but we'll guide you anyway.

Hungry Right Now?

Filter by time, ingredients you have, or just pick something random.
